transcript.transcript: Q. How did you get into Boise and stuff? My cousin told me to go buy a car, you know, and give me a ride and go to Boise. Oh, just buy a car in Boise? Yeah, this is my cousin, I can buy a car, you know. And after this, they buy the car because they want to come and move to California. Oh really? Yeah. Is that when the crops were almost down? Yeah, because it's about pretty close to finish the job over there. Oh, really? Yeah. Did Joe help you find a job in California, or was it just your brother? No, no. I go right to my cousin in Sanger, California, and say it's okay, there's a lot of jobs over there in California, and pay more money. Yeah, pay more money. Oh, really, more money. How much did you get paid in Idaho? In Idaho, they... Pretty cheap. Pay more money in California. Really? Because in Idaho, they make it, work for contract, you see. How many acres you make a day, you know. That wasn't too good. Well, A lot of people make good money. You work hard, you know, or fast, you know. You make money. About the camps, what kind of... What were the buildings made out of? The buildings, I don't know. The buildings I think were made a long time ago, you know. They're not real good because not really, really cold, you know. Uh-huh. Yeah, real... They're looking nice. Were they made out of wood? Were there... They're wood. Yeah, I think they're wood. Wood. Yeah, wood. They got showers and everything. Oh, really? Were there showers inside the... No, showers over there separate, you know, for the girls and for the men. Oh, Yeah. And that's where you take a shower. Take a shower. And there's a hot water. Yeah, the hot water, yeah. Yeah. Okay. Did they have flush toilets? Yeah. I don't remember the flush toilets, you know. I think in Acai, I don't remember. I think in Acai. Can you remember, was, Were the streets dirt? Well, in the middle of the Homedale, I think the streets got cement, you know, oil, cement or something. Uh-huh, but... Yeah, I don't know if it's okay. The camp was about a half mile? Pretty close, about half a mile, not too far, you know. Sometimes you walk to town. I'm not really sure where the camp is, but was it on... I think they're coming this, highway, they're coming to Marsing. Yeah. And I, yeah, I come back, see my uncle, because my uncle working in Marsing. They got another camp over there. And right now they got a new one now, you know. I know when I go over there in Idaho, see you one day, I see their camps, you know, real new ones, right there in Marsing. And still the Mexicans over there working. Really? Yeah. That's so awesome. Yeah. Is it at the same place where you work, the camp? Or are they moving? I don't know. Pretty close, same place, I think. But I don't see him in Homedale, maybe stay or maybe not. Oh, really? No, I don't go in Homedale. But I see him in Marsing, Marsing, Idaho. And I got a friend right then in Morrissey, Idaho. They're coming from the General. Yeah, my friend, me and this friend playing when young boys and mess. Oh, really? Yeah. So did he go out there with you to Idaho? Yeah, I see him a long time ago over there. Oh, really? Yeah, and this stay over there. Oh, really? Living over there, you know. That stay over there, living over there. Okay, did they have any like a Mexican commission in the camp or anything? Like, not some type of government or like, were there any people that set up all the dances and stuff like that? Yeah, somebody making the dances over there, you know, one people right there. In the camp? Yeah, working the camp, you know, they make the dances, you know. Right there, on their own. Did you notice, like anybody that came around to check the camp? Did you, did anybody come and check the camp? Oh yeah, Joe, you know the B-Boss come back and check everything. Something happened in the camp, you know, you called the B-Boss, you know, the name Joe. Do you know where the call went to? Huh? Do you know where the call went to? Where you called? Yeah, you go and call, you know, and come back, you know, come back every day. They got an office, I think, right there in the camp. Oh, did they? Yeah. And that was for complaints? Complaints, or you want something, or... Yeah. But I like these men because I helped you real good, you know. This boss, the name Joe. Still living, I don't know, maybe die or not, but I still live in Ritena and Jandel, you know. Real tall guy. Real good man. I don't remember. What was Joe like? Well, Joe, I think like the Mexican, because the Mexican make good job. And when I go over there, you know, me and my brother real happy to the big boss Joe because a real good friend to everybody. Real good man. Yeah, helping everybody. Do you remember if he had a title or anything like, was he just a big boss or did he have like, was he foreman or anything like that? Well, this Joe is a tele-deforeman, what you do, you know, than other foreman. Oh really? Yeah. Joe, I think, working for the rancheros. Associacion de Rancheros, I think, I don't know. And, real good man, you know, and talk to you real nice. Really? You knew Spanish? Yeah, I knew Spanish, yeah. This Joe, when coming to Braceros, you know, go to Mexico and go contract the Braceros to bring it to Idaho. Long time ago. Name's... I don't know if I have it here... Maybe, I don't know, I owe Joe right now, maybe. Really? Maybe too old, I don't know. Well, I mean, back then that's what you were supposed to do. They tell me that they be, they got a, in Idaho, I think, after there, I come back, you know, as the B-man right there in Homedale, you know. Oh, really? Maybe no work no more for for this Mexican, or Rancheros Associacion, I don't know. That was a big thing, the Rancheros Association. Yeah. With all the ranchers together. Yeah, all the ranchers together, yeah. And Joe was the link between the ranch and the ranch. Yeah, and the ranches, yeah. Did you ever go into Joe's office or anything? Yeah, they got a new office right there in the camp, you know, and you want to go complain for something, you tell them, you know. And he's a good man because I like and I remember a real good man. You were, what, 19? The 1955, I worked over there. He was 22. Oh, he was 22. Yeah, 22. 1955. And Joe helped all the people, like in Marsing and you know... I don't know about Marsing, but in Homedale, you know, Joe was the big boss. I don't know, I don't remember Joe is the B-boss. I don't know if you remember Joe Gomarcy or Caldwell or Nampa. I'm trying to find the name of that guy that I have. Okay, when you went to the camp, Was it hard to find like how to live and stuff? Yeah a little bit sometimes. They need to, when after they work you're supposed to go cook you know and they need to put on the stove, you know, warm the stove, you know, and they know how to water them. They need you put on, the cold water put on hot, you know, for watch the beaches. Was it just a little stove? No, big ones, you know. Oh, really? Real nice. Did you have it inside your cabin? No, inside the kitchen. Oh, was there a kitchen in the cabin? Yeah, kitchen and a cabin for you to sleep. The kitchen and room for sleep. It was together though? Yeah, together. In each cabin? Yeah. Oh. Did everybody have to cook their own stuff? Yeah, everybody got to use the stove, you know, every room. Uh-huh. Yeah, every room, yeah. Did one of you people that stayed in your cabin cook, do the cooking, or did everybody take turns? No, but four guys, four or five, you know, you cook together, you know. Uh-huh. Yeah. Living together. Did you only work in Idaho for just one summer or something? Yeah, for one whole year. And after that, I go to California because they tell me it's too cold over there in the wintertime. A lot of snow. Who told you that? The people. I think these guys living over there, you know, in Idaho, they say, Oh, I stay here in the wintertime, this is too cold, you know. Mexican? Yeah. And after, me and my brother said, we better move to California. Uh-huh. And you went there? Yeah. Oh. Did you, Did it ever really get cold in the morning there? Yeah, a little bit in the morning, yeah. Did they give you a lot of blankets? No, they didn't buy the blankets. Oh, really? You bought your own? Yeah. For the cabin? Yeah. Did they give you the mattress? Yeah, the bed and everything. And you bought your own food too? Yeah. Is there anything else that you remember? I don't remember. I remember the way for the payday and go buy the groceries. Oh, really? Yeah. And they not go... My brother not drink too much. Uh-huh. They go in the camp and stay over there. Did a lot of, did any of the Mexicans drink a lot? Yeah, a lot of them were drinking all the time. Really? Yeah. After work. Yeah, after work, you know. Do you remember any names of the bars or anything like that? No, I remember one bar, I don't know, I think they call it Rock Bar or something. It was in Homedale? In Homedale. May not be true, but I remember the name, Rock Bar or something. All Mexicans go right there. Right. But yeah, I think, I remember they can't go in other bars because they'll tell you sorry. Really? That's the only one with the wrong bar? Yeah. They wouldn't let you drink if you went? No, in other bars you can't. Oh. Yeah. Did that make any Mexicans mad that you couldn't go anywhere else? Yeah, a lot of Mexican men because they can't go. They want to go in other bars or would say sorry. Were there any fights because of that or anything like that? No, no, not too many fights. And after, me and my brother said, we better move to California because I came to discrimination here and home there. Was there? A long time ago. Really? Yeah. You told me once that you couldn't find food or something or... Yeah, a lot of... Go to Caldwell one day, you know, and try to eat in the cafes and tell you sorry, you know. Really? That wasn't easy? And I real hungry, you know. And I go in other cafes and say sorry. Really? Did you find... I don't know why, you know. Did you finally find some food? After I go in one store, you know, Leo's store and buy something, you know, and go back to home there. Like if, okay, you said that if you had any problems you'd go talk to Joe, you know? Yeah. Like, what if you were in Caldwell, and you didn't have anywhere to go, what would you do? Would you call him or something? No, they need to go over there, you know. Really? Yeah, they can call, yeah. Really? Yeah. Do you have a secretary? I don't remember they got a secretary, I don't remember, yeah. I see Joe in the office all the time. Was there any tractors or anything up there? Did they have any tractors yet? Oh yeah, yeah, see the Rancheros got a tractor or something. And then you went behind a tractor or something when you worked there? No, this job, me and my girls don't, they don't need to use tractors. Getting back to when you came from Mexico, How was it when, like, how did you feel when you came into the new, in the United States? Did you feel... I feel okay, you know, because, when coming here, you know, it's, you buy something, you know, go eat or buy clothes, you know, more easy. Uh-huh. Yeah. You I remember I working one day I buy my shoes or Levi's you know I remember I buy Levi's for 75 cents a dollar you know in Texas. Really? Was it hard to get in Mexico or something? Well, over there they need to pay, work more days to buy shoes. Oh, really? Oh, I see. And I, in Texas, when I come back, I work all day for $4. Really? All day long. Yeah. Yeah, $4. With that bottle of stuff? No, no. They work, and when the sun come back, the sun go, you know, not for hours, you know, all day. All day, huh? Yeah. About how many hours? I don't know maybe about 12. Was it hard work? Not really hard sometimes. Did you have any trouble like with the new food or anything like that? No. Oh, just natural? No. About When did you start to learn English? Did you know English in Idaho a little bit? No, a little bit. Really? Yeah, a little bit. I talk English a little bit, not too much. In Idaho? Yeah, I started in California. Oh, really? Yeah. Did somebody teach it to you there? No, to my friends. Really? Yeah. I see. Yeah. Oh, I worked at the rancheros in California. Real nice people in California. Gee, I worked at these bosses over there. And real happy because they're nice to you. Uh-huh. Yeah. Like, did you leave Idaho, like, probably in about July, end of July? Yeah, I think in... I think in July. Did you go with your brother, with your cousins? Yeah, my brothers and come back to to Santa California. Uh-huh. How'd you get there? In a bus? In a bus, in the Reinhardt. Yeah. Mama. Yeah, Reinhardt. Go to the Hohendael, to I think, come back to Winnemucca, Reno, Sacramento, and go to Fresno. Yeah, I passed here to Ladderlack. All right, that was 1955. 1955, yeah. Did they just, how did they tie it up that the work was over? Did they just tie you or? Yeah, they still did a little bit of work over there. When my brother said, I think My brother said, I think go to California because there are more jobs over there. My cousin, my cousin, I write a letter and say, come and everybody, you know, I got a cousin in San Diego, California. And I'm going to go over there, you know, find a job and I started working. Did you? Yeah. Did your cousin help you? Yeah. How did they communicate from California to Idaho? They write one letter to my cousin. Oh, did you? Yeah. Did one of your brothers know how to write? Yeah. Did he write in Spanish? Yeah, in Spanish, yeah. Okay. Yeah. Then they knew it was time to go from Idaho. Yeah, they said come back, there's a lot of jobs here in California. I want to study over there. The next day I started working. Really? Yeah, the next day I started working. Really? Yeah, a lot of jobs in San Diego, California. Did you have any money saved from Idaho when you went? Yeah, I did. And sent it to Mexico. Oh, did you send it to your mom? Yeah, I sent it to my mother all the time. Was Idaho different? All the time, yeah. Yeah. Was... I know, was Idaho different or... No, the Idaho is not bad, you know. In California, they feel more, more happy because in Fresno and Sango you know a lot of Mexicans and you go all over and dancing and eat a lot of Mexican cafes or Americans you know go all over over there, no discrimination. But Idaho is different. Yeah, Idaho is different. Long time ago, I don't know right now. Long time ago, it's a little bit different. In California, you go all over. You go in a Mexican cafe or American, you know, you go. It's okay. But there wasn't that much to do in Idaho then? Was it kind of boring? Yeah. Not just work and then... Yeah, work and then say, more better move in some place. Really? Yeah. Yeah. But I like working in Idaho too, you know, I guess that's about it. Was there anything to do in the camps or in Idaho? Did they have a game room or a full table? No, no, I don't got nothing, nothing. Uh-huh. Now they, they ask for the work, you know, they got a little bakery or go buy coke or something, go drink coke. Uh-huh. Or somebody want to go buy beer or somebody want to drink and go buy and bring in the camp and drink but my brothers never go buy beer, you know? Yeah. No. This and after the work you go take a child and eat and after you go drink one coke, you know. Uh-huh. Yeah, right there in the camp, yeah. They got a bakery, a little store. Right. Yeah. And, did you, Is that all you did was just eat and then go to bed? Yeah. Did they have electricity in the camp? Light? Yeah, I think they got a light. I remember they got a light. Were there whole families in the camp? Yeah, you got a brother or cousin you want to live together for four or five, you know. Were there kids, or? Yeah, kids too, you know. A lot of families. Did they have a school for the kids? Yeah, they got a school, yeah. Oh, did they? But I didn't, I don't remember, I didn't go to Homedale School. Oh, did they go? Yeah, Hondal, because I don't remember the school and the camp. Oh, really? I didn't go to Hondal. And they went to school in the high school? Yeah. Did the kids learn English there? Yeah. Yeah, a lot of Mexicans in America, in Texans that talk good English. Yeah. Too many come in there, Mexico, you know, people there, fees and passport for work in the United States. And I remember Hondebo they got a small park by the Snake River. Sometimes, you know, Saturday or Sunday you go over there you know and sit down. Was Saturday and Sunday off? Sometimes work Saturdays, Sunday off. Sunday off all the time? Yeah. Did Joe check to make sure you didn't work on Sunday? A lot of people work Sundays. Oh, really? Do they work seven days? Yeah, sometimes work seven days because they make a contract they want to finish, you know? You mean they said they'd finish that field and they'd get paid for it? Yeah, they'd pay for it, you know? Oh, I see, okay. Yeah, you work with one ranchero, you know, and finish, you know, and after they give you the check to the boss, and after the boss give it to you. Oh, okay. Maybe the boss will take a little bit of money too. Really? I think, I don't know. Yeah. They didn't tell you though? No, I think the boss took the money, maybe the rancheros paid for it, I don't remember. Really? Yeah. Did they have any classes for you to learn English there? No, no, no. Did they, were there any Mexicans in the camp trying to read books or anything to learn? Yeah, a lot of guys books, you know. Yeah. How did they, how did you sign your name for your check? Did a lot of them sign it with an X or something? Or when you had to cash your check? Oh yeah, they give it to one, one check, you know, to me and my brother, you know, and they give you a big check, you know, go change it, you know, and ask to see how many you, you know. And you split it up? Yeah, split it up. But how did, did they make you sign it on the back? Yeah. Did you sign it? Yeah. Did anybody sign it with an X? No. No? No. They didn't sign it, yeah. Really? Yeah. Okay. And sometimes they give the check to everybody too. Oh really? Just to pad it on the river? Yeah. You were going to say something more. Yeah, and I remember when we finished the one ranch, you know, see how many acres, you know, and after they give it there. The boss, they check, you know, and after they give it to you, you know. About the kids, Did they have a separate family quarters for the kids or for the whole family? If there's kids in the family, did they get one cabin? Yeah, they live, no, the Mexicans, they got a kids, you know, they give the room and everything, you know, living right there, everybody. Sometimes you got two beds or three beds, you know. And everybody lived there? Yeah. They had a kitchen in there? Kitchen, yeah. So you had to use wood for the stove? Yeah, yeah. Okay. I think they need to go get the water outside, I don't remember, you know. Oh, really? Yeah. They have wells? No, they got this kind of... Bottle? Yeah. But outside you go get it, I remember. And they need warm in the water. Yeah. Yeah. Okay. And then they had dance and stuff like that. Yeah. And I think that... Not for sure, but I think the toilets are aside. Uh-huh. You know. Okay. Yeah. How many people do you think were there? Not for sure, maybe about 200 or 300. In the camp? Yeah. In one camp? Yeah, in one camp. They were just all from different parts of Mexico? Yeah. Texas? Yeah, Texas. Were there any babies born at the camp or did they take them to the hospital? Take them to the hospital. Really? But they were still born? Yeah. Okay. Well, I guess that's it. Thanks. Stop it.

lines.line: # Arrival in Boise

> The interview begins with the participant explaining how they came to Boise on a cousin’s advice to buy a car. The discussion touches on familial motivations and plans to move to California for better opportunities.
** Boise, cousin, car, California, move **

metadata.title: Arrival in Boise
metadata.synopsis: The interview begins with the participant explaining how they came to Boise on a cousin’s advice to buy a car. The discussion touches on familial motivations and plans to move to California for better opportunities.

lines.line: # Finding Work

> Discussion about finding work in California compared to Idaho. The participant describes the better pay in California and the contract-based work system in Idaho, emphasizing the economic motivations for the move.
** work, California, Idaho, pay, contracts **

metadata.title: Finding Work
metadata.synopsis: Discussion about finding work in California compared to Idaho. The participant describes the better pay in California and the contract-based work system in Idaho, emphasizing the economic motivations for the move.

lines.line: # Living Conditions in Camps
> The participant describes the housing and living conditions in the camps, which include wooden buildings, separate showers, and the amenities available for workers.
** camps, buildings, wood, showers, amenities **

metadata.title: Living Conditions in Camps
metadata.synopsis: The participant describes the housing and living conditions in the camps, which include wooden buildings, separate showers, and the amenities available for workers.

lines.line: # Facilities and Infrastructure
> Conversation about the facilities in the camps, including toilets and roads made of cement. The participant recalls the proximity of the camp to nearby towns and mentions other camps in the area.
** facilities, toilets, roads, proximity, towns **

metadata.title: Facilities and Infrastructure
metadata.synopsis: Conversation about the facilities in the camps, including toilets and roads made of cement. The participant recalls the proximity of the camp to nearby towns and mentions other camps in the area.

lines.line: # Social Life and Community

> The participant remembers social activities like dances organized within the camp and checks made by supervisors. The role of 'Joe', the camp boss, in managing and checking in on the camp is highlighted.
** social activities, dances, camp, Joe, supervision **
keywords: ** social activities, dances, camp, Joe, supervision **

metadata.title: Social Life and Community
metadata.synopsis: The participant remembers social activities like dances organized within the camp and checks made by supervisors. The role of 'Joe', the camp boss, in managing and checking in on the camp is highlighted.
metadata.keywords: social activities, dances, camp, Joe, supervision

lines.line: # Interactions with the Boss
> The participant elaborates on their interactions with Joe, the camp boss, describing him as a supportive figure who was involved in contracts and management between the ranch and workers.
** Joe, boss, supportive, foreman, management **

metadata.title: Interactions with the Boss
metadata.synopsis: The participant elaborates on their interactions with Joe, the camp boss, describing him as a supportive figure who was involved in contracts and management between the ranch and workers.

lines.line: # Cooking and Daily Routine
> A look into the day-to-day life in the camps, focusing on cooking arrangements, shared responsibilities among workers, and the stoves provided for meal preparation.
** cooking, daily life, routine, stoves, meals **
keywords: ** cooking, daily life, routine, stoves, meals **

metadata.title: Cooking and Daily Routine
metadata.synopsis: A look into the day-to-day life in the camps, focusing on cooking arrangements, shared responsibilities among workers, and the stoves provided for meal preparation.
metadata.keywords: cooking, daily life, routine, stoves, meals

lines.line: # Cold Idaho Winters and Move to California

> The participant describes the harsh winters in Idaho, leading to a decision to move to California for better weather and job opportunities. Challenges faced in Idaho such as buying blankets are discussed.
** Idaho, winters, move, California, blankets **

metadata.title: Cold Idaho Winters and Move to California
metadata.synopsis: The participant describes the harsh winters in Idaho, leading to a decision to move to California for better weather and job opportunities. Challenges faced in Idaho such as buying blankets are discussed.

lines.line: # Discrimination and Searching for Better Opportunities
> The conversation touches on discrimination faced by the participant in Idaho, leading to difficulties in accessing certain facilities and resulting in the choice to migrate to California.
** discrimination, Idaho, facilities, migration, California **

metadata.title: Discrimination and Searching for Better Opportunities
metadata.synopsis: The conversation touches on discrimination faced by the participant in Idaho, leading to difficulties in accessing certain facilities and resulting in the choice to migrate to California.

lines.line: # Adapting to Life in the United States

> The participant discusses initial experiences in the U.S., comparing the cost of living and ease of purchasing goods with life in Mexico. Reflections on work and shopping experiences in Texas are included.
** United States, adaptation, cost of living, Mexico, Texas **
keywords: ** United States, adaptation, cost of living, Mexico, Texas **

metadata.title: Adapting to Life in the United States

metadata.keywords: United States, adaptation, cost of living, Mexico, Texas

lines.line: # Life in Idaho and Return to California
> The participant reflects on life in Idaho, mentioning limited social activities at the camps and the eventual return to California, driven by family ties and better prospects.
** Idaho, life, social activities, return, California **
keywords: ** Idaho, life, social activities, return, California **

metadata.title: Life in Idaho and Return to California
metadata.synopsis: The participant reflects on life in Idaho, mentioning limited social activities at the camps and the eventual return to California, driven by family ties and better prospects.
metadata.keywords: Idaho, life, social activities, return, California

lines.line: # Family Life and Living Arrangements
> A discussion on the living arrangements for families within the camp including the facilities for cooking and daily activities. The participant remembers shared spaces for families and children.
** family, living arrangements, camp, cooking, children **

lines.line: # Conclusion
> The interview concludes with a brief mention of the amenities at the camp, the presence of families, and a note on births at the camp, wrapping up the participant’s shared memories.
** conclusion, amenities, camp, families, memories **

story.title: Gomez_Salomon_OH 2293 Side B
story.description: Recorded on April 10, 1982. Topic is Mexican-American immigration to Idaho.
